title: What Can You Insure?
subtitle: Insurable risks & types of cover

hook.metaphor: Not everything can sit under the umbrella. You can insure your house against fire, but you can't insure a lottery ticket against not winning, or your business against simply making a bad strategic bet. Some risks fit insurance; others don't. Knowing which is which tells you where the safety net can actually catch you — and where you're on your own.
hook.scenario: A company wants to insure three things: its warehouse against fire, itself against being sued by a customer, and its CEO's risky expansion strategy against 'not working out'. An insurer happily quotes the first two and laughs at the third. Why?

learn: For a risk to be **insurable**, it generally must be: **accidental/fortuitous** (not deliberate or certain), **measurable** (you can estimate the likelihood and cost), **spread across many** (poolable), and **not catastrophic to the insurer** (not everyone losing at once). The CEO's strategy fails several tests — it's a *deliberate bet* whose outcome isn't a random accident. That's **strategic risk** (its own track), which you manage, not insure.

This is why some risks are **uninsurable**: deliberate acts, normal business/strategic risk, and sometimes systemic catastrophes (war, or, increasingly, some climate and pandemic risks where everyone is hit together). The safety net has edges.

Within the insurable world, cover comes in recognisable types. **Property** insurance: physical assets against fire, theft, flood (the warehouse). **Liability** insurance: claims that you harmed others — public liability, product liability, professional indemnity (the customer lawsuit).

More specialised covers map to modern risks (and to other tracks): **Cyber** insurance (breaches, ransomware — Cyber & IT Risk), **Directors & Officers (D&O)** (protecting leaders personally from claims), and **Business Interruption (BI)** — covering lost income while you're shut down after an insured event (the link to Business Continuity).

And a subtle but vital point: insurers **price and exclude** based on these criteria. Cover comes with **exclusions** (things explicitly not covered) and conditions — which is why 'I have insurance' is never the whole story (the claims-denial lesson is coming). What you're *actually* covered for is defined by the policy's fine print, not its title.
bridge: So at work, you can quickly sort what the safety net can catch: *is this risk accidental, measurable, and poolable (insurable) — or a deliberate/strategic bet you must manage yourself (uninsurable)?* Then match real exposures to the right cover — property, liability, cyber, D&O, business interruption — and always check the *exclusions*, because the policy title promises far less than the policy fine print delivers.

interactions.title: Why won't they insure the strategy?
interactions.prompt: An insurer covers the warehouse and the lawsuit risk but refuses to insure the CEO's expansion strategy 'not working out'. Why?

options.label: Insurers are just being unhelpful.
options.feedback: It's not unhelpfulness — a strategic bet isn't an insurable risk. It's a deliberate decision whose outcome isn't a random, measurable accident, and it can't be pooled. That's strategic risk to manage, not insure.

options.label: Strategic bets aren't accidental, measurable, or poolable — they're uninsurable.
options.feedback: Exactly. Insurable risks are accidental, measurable, and spread across many. A chosen strategy fails those tests — it's strategic risk you manage, not transfer to an insurer.

options.label: The CEO should just demand they cover it.
options.feedback: No insurer will cover a deliberate business bet — it breaks the fundamentals of insurability. The strategy must be managed (Strategic Risk track), not insured away.

interactions.title: Match the cover to the risk
interactions.prompt: Match each type of insurance to what it covers.

pairs.right: Physical assets vs fire, theft, flood
Lost income while shut down after an insured event

teachBack: Explain it to a friend in one line: “You can insure risks that are accidental, measurable, and poolable — property, liability, cyber, D&O, business interruption — but not deliberate strategic bets; and what you're really covered for is defined by the exclusions, not the policy's title.”

checkpoint.q: For a risk to be insurable it generally must be…
options: Accidental, measurable, poolable, and not catastrophic to the insurer
checkpoint.explain: Those criteria are why a chosen strategy (deliberate, unique) is uninsurable while a fire is insurable.

options: Lost income while you're shut down after an insured event
checkpoint.explain: BI replaces income during downtime — the financial link to business continuity.

checkpoint.q: Why does 'I have insurance' never tell the whole story?
options: Cover is defined by the policy's exclusions and conditions, not its title
checkpoint.explain: The fine print — exclusions and conditions — determines what you're actually covered for.
